coredata.dc:title: Salt-induced gold nanoparticles aggregation lights up fluorescence of DNA-silver nanoclusters to monitor dual cancer markers carcinoembryonic antigen and carbohydrate antigen 125
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xin
Ji, Jiangrong
Chen, Panpan
Wu, Jiafeng
Jin, Yang
Zhang, Liying
Du, Shuhu
coredata.dc:description: In clinical diagnosis of cancer, the monitoring of single tumor marker may result in many false and missed results, while simultaneous detection of multiple tumor markers should be more accuracy and effective. Here, we report a new strategy that salt-induced gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) aggregation lights up fluorescence of dual-color DNA-silver nanoclusters-aptamer (DNA-AgNCs-apta) for the simultaneous monitoring of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125). The dual-color aptasensor system is composed of green-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CEA aptamer (gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1) and red-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CA125 aptamer (rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2) in the ratio of 1:1 in volume. Upon addition of AuNPs, gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and/or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 are flexibly adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs by terminal aptamer(s), which prevents salt-induced AuNPs aggregation under high salt condition and results in fluorescence quenching based on surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer (SPEET). With the addition of CEA and/or CA125, the target(s) and corresponding aptamer(s) coordinate to form the complex, keeping DNA-AgNCs-apta(s) far away from the surface of AuNPs and making AuNPs aggregated in high salt medium. The AuNPs aggregation leads to the recovery of fluorescence signals of DNA-AgNCs-apta(s) due to weakened SPEET. Utilizing the fluorescence aptasensor system, the limit of detection of CEA and CA125 are as low as 7.5 pg·mL-1 and 0.015 U·mL-1, respectively. The proposed method can be applied to the selective and simultaneous determination of CEA and CA125 in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles aggregation
DNA-Silver nanoclusters
Dual-color aptasensor
Simultaneous detection
CEA
CA125

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of CEA or CA125 in aqueous solution
Fluorescent detection of CEA and CA125 in the serum samples
The fluorescence sensing mechanism for CEA and/or CA125 assay
Sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method
Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, C) TEM images of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2, respectively. (B, D) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2, respectively. The inset is the corresponding color photos under UV lamp (a) and daylight (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the interaction between DNA-AgNCs-aptas and AuNPs in the absence (a) and the presence (b) of CEA and CA125, and the corresponding fluorescence responses.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNPs (a) and fluorescence emission spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (b) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (c). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (a), gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + AuNPs (b) and gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + AuNPs + CEA (c). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (a), rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + AuNPs (b) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + AuNPs + CA125 (c). Inset: photographs of the corresponding colors change under a 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 in different systems: gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (a), gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + AuNPs (b), gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + AuNPs + CEA (c), gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + CEA + AuNPs (d) and gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 + CEA (e). (B) Normalized fluorescence intensity of rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 in different systems: rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (a), rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + AuNPs (b), rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + AuNPs + CA125 (c), rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + CA125 + AuNPs (d) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 + CA125 (e). Inset: photographs of the corresponding colors changes under a 365 nm UV lamp. (AuNPs: 1.6 nM; CEA: 8 ng mL−1; CA125: 30 U mL−1). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A, C) Evolution of fluorescence spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (in presence of AuNPs and NaCl) with the increase of target amount, respectively. (B, D) The linear relationships between F/F0 and the concentrations of CEA (0–9 ng mL−1) and CA125 (0–50 U mL−1), respectively. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of targets.

(A, B) Schematic demonstration of synthesizing dual-color DNA-AgNCs-aptas sensor (gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2); (C) Pictorial representation of the fluorescence detection of CEA and CA125 by the use of the dual-color aptasensor and AuNPs.
Real detection and recovery test of CEA and CA125 in different human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Salt-induced gold nanoparticles aggregation lights up fluorescence of DNA-silver nanoclusters to monitor dual cancer markers carcinoembryonic antigen and carbohydrate antigen 125

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In clinical diagnosis of cancer, the monitoring of single tumor marker may result in many false and missed results, while simultaneous detection of multiple tumor markers should be more accuracy and effective. Here, we report a new strategy that salt-induced gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) aggregation lights up fluorescence of dual-color DNA-silver nanoclusters-aptamer (DNA-AgNCs-apta) for the simultaneous monitoring of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125). The dual-color aptasensor system is composed of green-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CEA aptamer (gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1) and red-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CA125 aptamer (rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2) in the ratio of 1:1 in volume. Upon addition of AuNPs, gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and/or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 are flexibly adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs by terminal aptamer(s), which prevents salt-induced AuNPs aggregation under high salt condition and results in fluorescence quenching based on surface plasmon enhanced energy transfer (SPEET). With the addition of CEA and/or CA125, the target(s) and corresponding aptamer(s) coordinate to form the complex, keeping DNA-AgNCs-apta(s) far away from the surface of AuNPs and making AuNPs aggregated in high salt medium. The AuNPs aggregation leads to the recovery of fluorescence signals of DNA-AgNCs-apta(s) due to weakened SPEET. Utilizing the fluorescence aptasensor system, the limit of detection of CEA and CA125 are as low as 7.5 pg·mL-1 and 0.015 U·mL-1, respectively. The proposed method can be applied to the selective and simultaneous determination of CEA and CA125 in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors of female reproductive organs worldwide. Its mortality rate is the highest among all kinds of gynecologic tumors. Most cases are diagnosed in the late stage due to the absence of specific symptom in the early stage, while the disease has metastasized beyond the primary site, resulting in the poor survival [1]. To decrease the mortality and morbidity, the detections of the various tumor markers (e.g., alpha fetoprotein (AFP), carbohydrate antigen 199 (CA199), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), and so on) show great significance for the pre-diagnosis and evaluation of ovarian cancer in early stage [2]. Among them, the detection of single tumor marker is in the majority, which is not sufficient enough for accurate diagnosis of cancer owing to the poor specificity between the only one biomarker and tumor. Therefore, simultaneous detection of dual/multiple tumor markers is considerably significant to improve the efficiency of detection and accuracy of diagnosis with the advantage of low sample consumption, low cost and rapid analysis [3–6]. To date, various methods have been reported to detect dual/multiple tumor markers for accurate diagnosis of ovarian cancers [7–9].

CEA, a cell surface glycoprotein, is a broad-spectrum tumor marker, related to ovary, lung, liver, pancreas, breast, cervix and prostate cancers [10]. CA125, a surface antigen, is a specific marker for ovarian cancer [11]. Consequently, it is beneficial to monitor CEA in the early diagnosis of ovarian, in combination with CA125. Now, numerous efforts have been made to simultaneously detect two tumor biomarkers. Main methods include colorimetric immunoassay [12], liquid-phase immunoassay [13], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS)-based immunoassay [14], electrochemical immunoassay [15], and so on. Although these assays show good accuracy, they may have some disadvantages of high cost, time-consuming and require tedious instrument operation. To overcome the above weaknesses, fluorescence aptasensors have captured tremendous attention due to the high selectivity, real-time detection and ease of operation [16].

At present, various types of nanoclusters have been employed to construct fluorescence biosensors for the determination of targets [17]. In particular, DNA stabilized silver nanoclusters (DNA-AgNCs) can emit broad-range tunable fluorescence from visible to near-IR by altering base types, base length, sequence and secondary structure of DNA template [18]. Furthermore, the nanomaterials also have many good features such as lower toxicity, higher fluorescence quantum yield and excellent photophysical property/stability/biocompatibility [19,20]. Thus, DNA-AgNCs are usually applied as label-free fluorescence probes to detect metal ion [21], small biological molecules [22], nucleic acid [23] and protein [24].

Aptamer as artificial oligonucleotides (DNA or RNA), manufactured through an in vitro process or systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) [25], exhibits high specificity and affinity for targets. In particular, it is easily modified at specific locations in the DNA sequence by chemical synthesis [26,27]. Therefore, fluorescence aptasensors based on DNA-AgNCs have attract enormous interest in the assay of biomolecules. Fang [28] designed a label-free DNA-AgNCs/aptamer probe based on hybridization inducing fluorescence enhancement to detect prostate-specifc antigen. Xu [29] had successfully established a simple and label-free method for detection of ATP based on two aptamer-DNA-AgNCs with the limits of detection was 0.44 and 0.65 mM, respectively. Liu [30] developed a DNA-AgNCs transfer-based strategy with aptamer for detection of membrane proteins. Shamsipur [31] reported an aptamer bioconjugated AgNCs to monitor apoptosis successfully. Although these aptasensors have been extensively developed, it is still a challenge to integrate with multicolored DNA-AgNCs for simultaneous and supersensitive detection of dual/multiple tumor markers.

In this paper, we report a “light-up” dual-color fluorescence aptasensor based on salt-induced gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) aggregation for ultrasensitive detection of dual tumor markers CEA and CA125. The dual-color fluorescence aptasensor is constructed by a simple mixing of green-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CEA aptamer (gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1) and red-emitting DNA-AgNCs with CA125 aptamer (rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2) in the ratio of 1:1 in volume. The two kinds of DNA-AgNCs-aptas were synthesized by NaBH4 mediating reduction of Ag ions in the presence of different DNA oligonucleotides, respectively (Scheme 1 A and B). After successive addition of AuNPs and high salt solution, gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and/or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 can be effectively adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs through electrostatic interaction, preventing AuNPs aggregation under high salt condition. As a result, fluorescence signals of DNA-AgNCs-aptas are efficiently quenched by AuNPs due to surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) between DNA-AgNCs-aptas and AuNPs. With addition of CEA and/or CA125, the corresponding aptamer can specifically bind the analytes, forming the complex that exfoliates DNA-AgNCs-aptas from the surface of AuNPs, leading to AuNPs aggregation and fluorescence recovery of DNA-AgNCs-aptas because the SPEET efficiency decreases (Scheme 1C). The dual-color fluorescence aptasensor can be utilized for the simultaneous monitoring of CEA and CA125 in serum samples of healthy humans and ovarian cancer patients.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4•2H2O) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were purchased from Shantou Xilong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shantou, China). All chemicals used were of analytical grade without any further purification. Dopamine (DA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA), glucose oxidase (GOD), thrombin (TT) and CEA were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CA125 was obtained from Kemer Biotech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared by a Millipore water purification system (Milfords, MA, USA). All the oligonucleotide sequences (see Table S1) used in this study were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Inc., and purified through high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

Two patients suffered from ovarian cancer (from the First Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing Medical University) and two healthy volunteers with informed consent forms supplied suitable serum samples. All samples were stored at −70 °C until analysis.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded by Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence instrument (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. A JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) instrument (JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize nanoparticles. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern, UK) particle size analyzer was used to measure the hydrodynamic sizes of nanoparticles. A FM-4P-TCSPC lifetime and steady state fluorescence spetrofluorometers (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA) was utilized to record time-resolved decay curves of DNA-AgNCs. A Canon 600D digital camera (Canon, Japan) was used to take fluorescent photos under AGL-9406 UV lamp (AGL, China). To verify the accuracy of the approach, serum samples were also analyzed with ELISA.

DNA-AgNCs-aptas were prepared according to the previously reported work with slight modification [32]. In brief, DNA1-aptamer1 or DNA2-aptamer2 strand (see the detailed composition in Table S1) was first dissolved into sodium phosphate buffer solution (10 mM Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4 and 10 mM MgCl2•6H2O, pH 7.4). Then, 40 μL of DNA1-aptamer1 or DNA2-aptamer2 solution (50 μM) was mixed with 60 μL of freshly-prepared AgNO3 solution (0.2 mM), followed by vigorously shaking for 30s. The mixture was incubated in the dark at room temperature for 15 min. Subsequently, 60 μL of freshly-prepared NaBH4 solution (0.2 mM) was introduced into the above mixture, vigorously shaking for 30s and kept in dark to react overnight at room temperature to obtain gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2. Finally, the as-obtained gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 was stored at 4 °C for future use.

AuNPs were synthesized according to the previous literature [33]. In typical, 5 ml of HAuCl4 (25 mM) was mixed with 120 mL of ultrapure water and the mixing solution was heated to boiling under vigorous stirring, followed by quickly adding 12.5 mL of trisodium citrate solution (38.8 mM). The reaction system was continuously stirred for 30 min to form the wine colloidal solution. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain AuNPs colloid. The AuNPs concentration is 12 nM, which is measured by UV method. Finally, the as-obtained AuNPs were stored at 4 °C for the following use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of CEA or CA125 in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 10 μL of AuNPs colloid and 12 μL of NaCl solution (1 M) were first added into 10 μL of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 solution, respectively. Total volume was completed to 120 μL with sodium phosphate (PBS) buffer solution. Then, the mixture was gently shaked and incubated at 25 °C for 5 min. Various concentrations of CEA solution were separately added into the above mixture and incubated at 25 °C for 20 min with the help of a reciprocating oscillator. Lastly, the fluorescent spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 were recorded with a spectrometer, and the signal readout values were calculated by the relative fluorescence intensity [F/F0], in which F and F0 represent the fluorescent intensities in the presence and absence of CEA (λex = 460 nm and λem = 530 nm), respectively. Meanwhile, CA125 was also measured with rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 using the same method, while varying the excitation and emission wavelength (λex = 540 nm and λem = 627 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of CEA and CA125 in the serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples (1–2 μL) were analyzed as above-mentioned measurement of CEA or CA125 in aqueous solution by adjusting the excitation wavelength of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2, respectively. Meanwhile, the accuracy of the proposed method was performed through a standard-spiked recovery experiment. Known different concentrations (low, medium and high) of standard CEA and CA125 were separately added into each serum sample and determined according to the above experiment conditions. For each concentration, the tests were repeated 5 times.

As shown in Table S1, DNA1 with 22 bases and DNA2 with 16 bases are used to stabilize AgNCs, respectively. Two kinds of DNA-AgNCs-aptas were synthesized in the molar ratio of 1:6:6 (DNA/Ag+/NaBH4). In Fig. 1 A and C, the typical TEM images show that the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 are dispersed and possess spherical shape with an average particle diameter of ∼6 nm and ∼7 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1B and D, the maximum excitation/emission wavelength of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 are 460 nm/530 nm and 540 nm/627 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the two kinds of DNA-AgNCs-aptas exhibit green and red fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, respectively, while appearing as colorless under daylight (inset in Fig. 1B and D). Taken together, the results indicate that the structural difference of DNA sequences from DNA-AgNCs determines the fluorescent emission properties of AgNCs. In Fig. S1, the citrated-stabilized AuNPs are monodispersed due to the negative electrostatic repulsion [34] and have an average size of 13 nm (Fig. S1A). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis gives the hydrate particle sizes of ∼30 nm for AuNPs (Fig. S1B), which are slightly larger than the sizes themselves due to the formation of hydration layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence sensing mechanism for CEA and/or CA125 assay
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the fluorescence sensing mechanism for the detection of CEA and CA125 by using DNA-AgNCs-aptas (gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2) together with AuNPs. In the absence of analytes (Fig. 2a), the aptamers (single strand DNA, ssDNA) at the 3′-end of DNA-AgNCs can electrostatically adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs [35] to maintain the AuNPs monodispersed in high-salt condition (see the TEM images of Fig. S2A), resulting in the fluorescence quenching of DNA-AgNCs-aptas due to SPEET [36]. In order to better understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we further investigated the fluorescence lifetime of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2. As shown in Fig. S3, the average decay time for gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 is calculated as 1.84 ns or 4.90 ns, respectively. Upon the addition of AuNPs, the decay time of the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 or rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 gradually decreases and finally reduces to 0.79 ns or 1.69 ns, respectively. In addition, the Stern-Volmer (SV) quenching constants were measured based on gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 versus concentration of AuNPs at three temperatures (288, 298 and 308 K). The SV equation is depicted as F0/F = 1+ Ksv C (C is the concentration of quencher AuNPs, Ksv is the SV quenching constants, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the DNA-AgNCs-aptas without and with AuNPs, respectively). Fig. S4 shows a negative relationship between Ksv and temperature, suggesting that the quenched process of DNA-AgNCs-aptas by AuNPs is static [37]. However, in presence of analytes (Fig. 2b), the corresponding aptamer can specifically bind the analytes to form complex, and DNA-AgNCs-aptas are far away from the surface of AuNPs, resulting in the gradual aggregation of AuNPs under the high-salt condition (Fig. S2B), accompanying with the visible color changes from wine-red to grey under daylight (inset in Fig. S2). Therefore, the fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs-aptas can be recovered owing to the weakened SPEET efficiency.

Moreover, the above mechanism for determination of CEA and CA125 was also confirmed by the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs displays quite wide absorption at 520 nm, which overlaps with the fluorescence excitation spectra of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 satisfying the requirement of SPEET between DNA-AgNCs (donor) and AuNPs (acceptor) [36]. When aptamer adsorbs onto the surface of AuNPs, it can be observed that the fluorescence quenching of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (curve b in Fig. 3B) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (curve b in Fig. 3C) due to SPEET [38,39]. Meanwhile, the color of the system changes from green or red to colorless under the UV lamp, respectively (photo b of the inset in Fig. 3B and C). In presence of CEA and CA125, the fluorescence of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (curve c in Fig. 3B) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (curve c in Fig. 3C) can be recovered owing to the weakened SPEET efficiency. The corresponding color variation from colorless to green or red under the UV lamp is also observed (image c of the inset in Fig. 3B and C).

To obtain optimized assay conditions for CEA and CA125, the AuNPs concentration, quenching response time of DNA-AgNCs-aptas to AuNPs, incubation temperature, pH value of the system, NaCl concentration and incubation time were investigated in detail. The corresponding data are given in Figs. S5–8. As shown in Figs. S5–8, the optimal AuNPs concentration, quenching response time, incubation temperature, pH value, NaCl concentration and incubation time are 1.6 mg·mL-1, 5 min, 25 °C, 7.4, 100 mM and 20 min, respectively.

To further demonstrate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method, the interaction of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1/rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 with AuNPs and CEA/CA125 were studied by fluorescent spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 4 A, upon the addition of AuNPs, the strong fluorescence signal of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (curve a) is rapidly quenched (curve b) due to SPEET between gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and AuNPs. After the addition of CEA, the fluorescence signal is recovered (curve d) due to the formation of aptamer-target complex, which separates gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 from the surface of AuNPs. However, when the adding order of AuNPs and CEA is reversed, that is to say, CEA is dropped into the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 system before addition of AuNPs. The efficiency of fluorescence recovery is slightly lower (curve c) than that of the former procedure (curve d). Additionally, after the only addition of CEA into the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 system, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreases (curve e), suggesting that the target itself hardly influences the fluorescence signal of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1. These results prove that the fluorescence recovery of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 is attributed to the presence of target CEA. For CA125, the similar results are also obtained (Fig. 4B). Thus, the proposed method for the detection of CEA and CA125 is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the sensitivity measurement, we first investigated the stabilities of the fluorescent aptasensor system. As shown in Figs. S9A and B, the fluorescent intensities of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 (at 530 nm) and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 (at 627 nm) are stable within 2 h, respectively, indicating the dual-color fluorescent aptasensor has high stability. Moreover, after being quenched by AuNPs, the fluorescent intensities of the dual-color fluorescent aptasensor keep stable within 60 min (Figs. S9C and D), ensuring a reliable reference signal for the analysis of targets.

Under the above optimal experimental conditions, the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 is used to quantitative detection of target CEA. As shown in Fig. 5 A, it is found that the fluorescence intensity of the gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 gradually increased with the increment of CEA concentration (from 0.01 to 9.0 ng·mL-1). Fig. 5B exhibits the linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio F/F0 and the concentration of CEA in the range from 0.01 to 0.9 ng·mL-1. The linear regression equation is y = 1.1938x + 1.0492 with the regression coefficient (R2) of 0.9952. The limit of detection (LOD) is 7.5 pg·mL-1 based on the 3σ/S calculation (σ is the standard deviation of the black (n = 10), and S is the slope of the linear regression equation). Similarly, for the sensitivity of rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 to CA125, the linear range is also obtained between 0.01 and 2.0 U·mL-1, the corresponding linear regression equation is y = 0.5937x + 1.0404 (R2 = 0.9917) with a LOD of 0.015 U·mL-1 (3σ/S) (Fig. 5C and D). Since the contents of CEA and CA125 in normal human serum are usually at the level of ∼5.0 ng·mL-1 [40] and ∼35 U·mL-1 [41], respectively, indicating that the dual-color fluorescent aptasensor should be sufficient for the practical clinical applications.

Meanwhile, the selectivity of the proposed method was investigated by observing its response to other nonspecific biomolecules or proteins such as DA BSA, OVA, GOD and TT under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S10, the additions of DA, BSA, OVA, GOD and TT to the assay system barely display influence, indicating that CEA and CA125 can specifically bind with the corresponding DNA aptamer without being influenced by other non-specific molecule and proteins. Thus, the proposed method can provide an excellent selectivity to detect CEA and CA125.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of CEA and CA125 in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the above results, the dual-color aptasensor system (fabricated by simply mixing gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2 with the 1:1 volumn ratio) was applied for the simultaneous analysis of CEA and CA125 in real serum samples (collected from two patients with ovarian cancer and two healthy volunteers) by adjusting the excitation wavelength of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2, respectively. The data shown in Table 1 suggest the obtained results are highly consistent with those from the ELISA method. Meanwhile, the recoveries of CEA and CA125 are achieved in the range of 98%–104%, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 5%, indicating that the proposed method has the excellent precision and reliability, which satisfy the demand of the clinical application. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1, the concentrations of CEA and CA125 in serum samples from ovarian cancer patients are obviously higher than those from normal humans, which give a distinct judgement whether or not women suffer from ovarian cancer.

The above results have verified the feasibility of the proposed method for the simultaneous determination of CEA and CA125. As shown in Table S2, compared with other works, the LOD of proposed method is lower than those of chemiluminescent immunoassay [42], fluoroimmunoassay [43] and electrochemical immunoassay [15]. Even though dual-responsive (colorimetric and electrochemical) immunoassay [12] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)-based multiplex immunoassay [14] have higher sensitivity than the proposed method, they require sophisticated procedures or large-scale instrument, limiting the rapid detection. Moreover, other methods for the assay of single CEA [44–46] or CA125 [47–49] also have supplied lower LOD or broad linear range, but our proposed method can simultaneously detect of CEA and CA125 (Table S2). In short, the approach proposed herein is flexible, simple, rapid, and is sufficient to simultaneously monitor CEA and CA125 in human serum.

In summary, we have successfully developed a novel method based on that salt-induced AuNPs aggregation lights up the fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs-aptas to simultaneously assay CEA and CA125. The merits of the as-developed method mainly contain the following three keypoints. (1) the DNA-AgNCs-aptas can adsorb the surface of AuNPs to display a stronger fluorescence quenching effect and the corresponding aptamers can specifically bind the targets to exhibit fluorescence recovering effect; (2) the simultaneous analysis of CEA and CA125 can been performed by adjusting the excitation wavelength of gDNA1-AgNCs-apta1 and rDNA2-AgNCs-apta2; and (3) the as-developed method can be extended to simultaneously examine multiple tumor markers by designing different DNA sequences composed of the corresponding aptamer and DNA template of AgNCs. In addition, the assay technique is very simple and rapid, compared with the reported methods, and each examination only needs a droplet 1–2 μL of serum sample. Therefore, the practicable and sensitive fluorescence aptasensor has significant prospects to be applied in clinical early diagnosis and treatment.

Xin Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jiangrong Ji: Data curation, Investigation. Panpan Chen: Data curation, Validation. Jiafeng Wu: Software, Data curation. Yang Jin: Resources, Visualization. Liying Zhang: Supervision, Methodology. Shuhu Du: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.05.027.
